1.summary: An election is when people vote to choose their representatives. In UK general elections you must be 18 or over to vote.
keyPoints: Voting is one way citizens take part in democracy.
MPs are elected to represent areas called constituencies.
A ballot paper lists the candidates you can vote for.
1.explainLike: An election is like choosing a class rep, everyone eligible gets one vote to pick who speaks for them.

2.summary: UK general elections use first-past-the-post (FPTP). The candidate with the most votes in each constituency wins a seat in the House of Commons.
keyPoints: Register to vote before election day, registration is a citizen responsibility.
A general election usually happens at least every five years.
The party with most MPs usually forms the government.
2.explainLike: FPTP means you do not need half the votes to win, just more than anyone else in your area.

3.summary: Elections connect citizens to Parliament. Voters choose MPs; MPs scrutinise government and can vote on laws.
keyPoints: Turnout measures what percentage of registered voters actually voted.
Safe seats have predictable winners; marginal seats are closely fought.
Local elections and devolved elections use different rules and ages in some nations.
3.explainLike: Your vote picks an MP, but the whole country's results together decide who runs the government.

4.summary: The electoral system affects representation. FPTP tends to produce majority governments but can under-represent smaller parties nationally.
keyPoints: A hung parliament occurs when no party wins an overall majority of seats.
Coalition or confidence-and-supply arrangements may follow a hung parliament.
Electoral reform debates compare FPTP with proportional systems such as STV or list PR.
4.explainLike: FPTP is simple to understand, but a party can win many votes nationwide and still win few seats.

5.summary: Citizenship requires explaining how elections uphold democracy and describing barriers to participation such as apathy or disinformation.
keyPoints: Compulsory voting exists in some countries but not the UK, participation is voluntary.
Voter ID requirements (where applied) aim to reduce fraud but may affect access.
Young people can influence politics before 18 through campaigns, petitions, and youth councils.
5.explainLike: Democracy needs informed voters, citizenship is about using your voice even when you cannot yet mark a ballot.

6.summary: Discuss advantages and disadvantages of lowering the voting age to 16, using evidence on maturity, tax, and participation.
keyPoints: Scotland allowed 16-17-year-olds to vote in some referendums and devolved elections.
Arguments for: taxation without representation, engagement in citizenship education.
Arguments against: concerns about independence of choice and consistency UK-wide.
6.explainLike: A discuss answer needs a benefit, a drawback, and a reasoned view, not just your personal opinion.

7.summary: Apply mark-scheme thinking: explain two ways elections promote accountability (AO2) and evaluate one limitation of FPTP (AO3).
keyPoints: Accountability: MPs can be voted out; manifestos set expectations; opposition challenges policy.
FPTP limitation: wasted votes in safe seats; disproportionate seat share vs vote share.
Use command words precisely, 'explain' needs because/therefore chains.
7.explainLike: Examiners credit developed points, identify the idea, then explain why it matters for democracy.

8.summary: Analyse how media, social media, and party machinery shape electoral outcomes and voter behaviour in the UK context.
keyPoints: Marginal seat targeting concentrates campaign spending and visits.
Digital micro-targeting raises questions about privacy and manipulation.
Evaluate whether reforms (spending caps, impartiality rules) balance free speech and fair elections.
8.explainLike: Modern elections are fought online as well as on doorsteps, analyse both the opportunities and risks.

9.summary: Evaluate the extent to which UK elections deliver legitimate democratic representation in the 21st century, integrating devolution and reform debates.
keyPoints: Legitimacy links to turnout, fairness of system, and public trust in results.
Compare FPTP stability with PR arguments for fairer reflection of views.
Conclude with a justified line, 'to a large extent' needs evidence on both sides.
9.explainLike: Mastery answers weigh systemic design, participation trends, and media influence before a precise evaluative conclusion.
